label: Intentional Stance
definition: Daniel Dennett's account (1971, 1987) of how we predict and explain behavior by treating a system as a rational agent with beliefs and desires, a strategy whose success, not the system's inner stuff, is what makes it correct to call the system a believer.

body.text: The intentional stance is a strategy for predicting and explaining behavior, named and developed by the philosopher Daniel Dennett, first in his 1971 paper "Intentional Systems" and most fully in his 1987 book "The Intentional Stance". Dennett distinguishes three stances one can take toward a system. From the physical stance you predict behavior from physical law and the system's material constitution. From the design stance you predict it from what the system was built or evolved to do, the way you predict an alarm clock will ring without knowing its circuitry. From the intentional stance you predict behavior by treating the system as a rational agent: you ascribe to it the beliefs and desires it ought to have given its situation and goals, and then predict that it will act rationally on them. To predict a chess program's move, you do not trace its transistors; you assume it wants to win, believes the rules, and will choose a good move.

Dennett's central and deflationary claim is that being a believer just is being reliably predictable from the intentional stance. There is no further metaphysical fact, no inner glow of "real" intentionality, that a system must possess on top of being usefully described this way. A system is a genuine intentional system if and to the degree that the stance works on it, if treating it as rational yields better predictions than the other stances afford. This makes intentionality, the aboutness of mental states, a matter of interpretation grounded in real patterns of behavior rather than an intrinsic, all-or-nothing property hidden inside the skull. Mental-state talk earns its keep as a powerful predictive heuristic, and that, for Dennett, is all it needs to be true.

The position is a form of mild realism. Dennett insists the patterns the stance latches onto are real, not arbitrary projections; a stance that did not track anything would not predict well. But the patterns are real in the way a center of gravity is real: indispensable, objective, yet not a further concrete thing over and above the system's parts. This places the intentional stance close to functionalism, since what matters is the role and the pattern rather than the substrate, while remaining distinct from any view that locates intentionality in a special inner ingredient.

The main objection comes from John Searle and others who hold that the stance captures only "as if" intentionality, a useful fiction, and misses original intentionality, the genuine aboutness that a conscious mind has and a thermostat lacks. On this view Dennett has changed the subject: he has explained when it is useful to talk about beliefs, not whether the system really has them, and the chinese-room argument is meant to show that no amount of predictively apt behavior adds up to understanding. A second worry is that the stance seems to make believerhood observer-relative and graded, which sits uneasily with the intuition that either a thing has a mind or it does not. Dennett embraces the gradedness as a feature, not a bug.

For AI the intentional stance is unusually practical. It licenses, and explains the success of, the everyday habit of describing systems in mentalistic terms: this model "thinks" the answer is X, "wants" to complete the pattern, "believes" the prompt. Dennett's framework says such talk is neither sloppy nor metaphorical confusion; it is the intentional stance working, and it works precisely because the systems instantiate real, rationality-approximating patterns. The framework also reframes the loaded question "does the AI really have beliefs?" as the more tractable question "how good and how stable is the intentional-stance description of it?" That move matters for assigning capabilities, for safety analysis where predicting an agent's goal-directed behavior is the whole game, and increasingly for questions of moral-patienthood, since if mindedness is a matter of degree and pattern rather than a binary essence, the line between mere tool and genuine agent becomes something we read off behavior rather than something settled in advance.
